posts.name: Roy Piper
posts.cooked:
Brilliant move. Can see all sorts of upside for Constellation.

They can increase production a lot because Schrader now has access to 500 acres of ToKalon. Their production price drops because they won’t have the fruit costs, as they can go in-house. As the old contracts ends with Andy, they won’t be subject to the new 175x bottle price for fruit. And they can remove the “Beckstoffer” from the label and just call it “ToKalon.” Not to mention Constellation has even better sections of ToKalon than Beckstoffer. Finally, they won’t be subject to the tonnage reductions all of the other clients in TK will have to deal with as the vineyard goes through its replant.

Exclusive: Constellation Buys Napa Cabernet Star Schrader Cellars | Wine...

Deal gives drinks giant a gleaming Napa Valley jewel for its fine wines crown

Drinks giant Constellation Brands has purchased Napa Valley cult winery Schrader Cellars, founder Fred Schrader told Wine Spectator. The blockbuster deal gives Constellation one of the elite names in Napa Cabernet, known for its Beckstoffer To Kalon Vineyard cuvée and several other notable bottlings.

posts.name: Arnold Caplan
posts.cooked:
We hope this note finds you well and enjoying a wonderful start to the summer. In the Napa Valley, our growing season began right on time with beautiful warm, dry days that are ideal for flower set. While it is a little too early to provide feedback, all indications are that very good things will come our way for harvest.

As we head into our 18th season at Schrader, we reflect with great pride on what we have achieved together and we are excited for the path ahead.

2000 was our first harvest from the Beckstoffer To Kalon Vineyard and we began with a brilliant young man who had never before made a bottle of Cabernet Sauvignon. Now, Thomas Brown is the most critically celebrated winemaker in America and the youngest to receive a perfect 100-point score from Robert Parker, Wine Advocate, and the only one to receive the same ‘perfect’ score from James Laube, Wine Spectator, via the blind tasting review program.

Our commitment to winemaking excellence began with a single block of clone 4 Cabernet in the heart of the Beckstoffer To Kalon Vineyard, and it has carried forward through nearly two decades to include what we believe are the finest sections of the Beckstoffer Vineyards in Oakville, Rutherford and St. Helena. Our goal, always, has been to bring you the very finest wine experience possible.

Since beginning on this journey, it has been an honor to faithfully and diligently care for our wine program and do our personal best to serve our loyal customers.

Recently, we were presented with an exceptional opportunity that will allow us to bring forward greater resources to create the Schrader wines you love and enjoy. After thoughtful discussions with our winemaker, Thomas Brown, and heartfelt consideration, we have made the decision to move forward with Constellation Brands, the perfect steward for our wine program. We embark on this path together, along with Thomas at the winemaking helm, committed to our standard of excellence in wine and customer relations. We are excited for the possibilities that will be realized with this transition.

Thomas will continue to oversee the vineyard operations and make the wines at Outpost. We will continue to participate in brand development and welcome you at events and tastings, and Frank will continue to be the friendly voice you hear on the other end of the phone for customer service. This is a ‘work with’, not a hand off. We have spent the better part of our lives bringing forward this magical wine and we will continue to be part of its enduring success.

In the coming months, what changes will you see in your experience with Schrader — none. We will continue to do our very best and bring you our very best. The 2017 harvest is poised to be one for the record books and we are delighted to be cultivating it with our friends and now family at Constellation Brands.

If you have any questions about this news please refer to our ‘Questions You May Have’ page on our website that may help clarify your initial questions. For immediate assistance feel free to email us at info@schradercellars.com or call us at 707 942 1212.

As ever, we sincerely appreciate your faith in us and enthusiastic support of our wine program.

With warmth and cheer,

Carol, Fred & Thomas

posts.name: Peter Hickner
posts.cooked:
A quick look at the financials of Constellation is an eye opener as to the value of name brands in the drinks business.
Three things pop out immediately.

  1. They have a huge amount of debt as a result of buying those brands.
  2. Fully 60% of their net assets and Goodwill and Intangibles i.e. the cost of the brand names.
    As a corollary of the first two items, they actually have negative tangible net worth.
  3. The kicker comes in the profitability, up steadily over the last three years increasing from an enviable 14% to an astounding 20% of sales.

P Hickner

posts.name: John Morris
posts.cooked:
Roy Piper:

They would save enormous sums by going in house. Why pay $100,000 per acre for fruit when you can pay the cost of managing the vineyard at $15,000? Besides, they don’t sell fruit to others. It’s all in house already so there is no “subsidising.” It’s just being shifted from Mondavi Reserve going 3-tier at $150 to all direct sales of a lot of cases at $225. Not only will it save many dollars per bottle in cost but will also add many dollars of revenue per bottle. Not to mention there is no way to expand the brand while Beckstoffer ToKalon lowers production due to a decade of replants.

Constellation does not outsource.

If they can shift fruit from Mondavi to Schrader and get a much higher price for the finished wine from the same fruit, that makes perfect corporate sense. But I’m sure Constellation is not looking at this a way to lower Schrader’s cost of fruit by selling to it at an inside, i.e., below-market, price.

The problem with that is that it would mean that Constellation would not be maximizing the return on the capital invested in its vineyard asset. Public company CFOs pay a lot of attention to returns on capital and they scrutinize subsidies between businesses because it can distort the real profitability of business units and lead to bad investments.

You’re viewing this entirely from the winemaker’s perspective. Constellation may just be looking at Schrader as a way to maximize its prior investment in ToKalon.

posts.name: J a y H a c k
posts.cooked:
This is not a good thing. You can’t have any knowledge of business, finance or a capitalist economy without realizing this is not good for the consumer. And that’s from someone who has been on the list for 5 years or more. And I open it for very special occasions, like my son’s wedding and when my brother in law was named a university provost. It’s not a Tuesday pizza wine, but that’s ok.

Why is this bad? Let’s keep it simple. There is a pie. It has a certain size and value. Let’s call the pie Schrader. Schrader then joins with the evil empire. Let’s call the evil empire Constellation. when Constellation pays for Schrader, a portion of constellation’s pie goes out the door. I do not begrudge the Schrader family the receipt of that part of the pie, but it’s gone from the wine making process. Anyone who works in M&A knows that the acquirer attempts to make that up by “cost saves.” The problem is that cost saves tend to be focused on reducing employee expense and shutting down facilities. Everything I have seen about winery economics indicates that salary and benefits expense are not nearly as large a percentage of total expenses as in many other businesses. Constellation is going to need to recoup the value of the money that goes out the door somehow? What are the opportunities to do so? Raise prices? Blend in cheaper grapes? Reduce customer service? Reduce public interest donations? Sell more wine to Chinese buyers with too much money who will blend To Kalon with Coca Cola. None of those choices seem good for me, the consumer.

I am not optimistic. I may never get an Old Sparky allocation, but I can live without it. I do not see the concentration of the wine business as a good thing. I’m going to have to increase my buying so that my sons have wine stashed away because I am concerned that by the time they start to pay for their own, their choices will be Constellation, Bronco or KJ.

The most interesting part of this transaction is that Constellation is in the process of reintroducing the ToKalon winery/ Label. It appears that they are doubling down on ToKalon/ wine from ToKalon. It will be very interesting to see how this all shakes down.

Let’s not get confused by the words “from ToKalon.” Due to what I would consider slight of hand by Mondavi, ToKalon is not considered, at least by the US Patent and Trademark Office, to be a specific place where grapes are grown. ToKalon is a trademark owned by . . . Constellation Brands, which acquired the trademark, directly or indirectly, from the original trademark registrant, Robert Mondavi Winery Corporation. If Constellation wants to quadruple production of “ToKalon” Cabernet, all the have to do is buy some arsenic-laden insipid cabernet grapes from the Central Valley, vinify it into a reddish colored liquid that fits within the legal definition of wine, and slap a label on it that says ToKalon.

If you have any doubts about their plans to expand the use of the term, on March 24, 2017, Constellation filed to trademark “TO KALON VINEYARD COMPANY” and “TO KALON WINE COMPANY.”

Andy Beckstoffer may be replanting, and I have heard a variety of stories about his right to use the term ToKalon for one of his vineyards, but I do not know the correct story. I do know that his website terminology treats ToKalon as though it is a place where grapes are grown, but he may have a deal with the Mondavi Wine Company, now binding on Constellation, allowing him to use a trademark owned by someone else. There are other possible legal explanations, but it’s not my field. Maybe an intellectual property lawyer can butt in. Note that the Mondavi trademark (1988) pre-dates the acquisition of the vineyard by Beckstoffer (1993). The vineyard used to be owned by Beaulieu, according to Beckstoffer, but according to Cellartracker, one person owns (or used to own) a bottle of 1978 Robert Mondavi Winery Cabernet Sauvignon Reserve To-Kalon Vineyard.

The most interesting part of this transaction is that Constellation is in the process of reintroducing the ToKalon winery/ Label. It appears that they are doubling down on ToKalon/ wine from ToKalon. It will be very interesting to see how this all shakes down.

Let’s not get confused by the words “from ToKalon.” Due to what I would consider slight of hand by Mondavi, ToKalon is not considered, at least by the US Patent and Trademark Office, to be a specific place where grapes are grown. ToKalon is a trademark owned by . . . Constellation Brands, which acquired the trademark, directly or indirectly, from the original trademark registrant, Robert Mondavi Winery Corporation. If Constellation wants to quadruple production of “ToKalon” Cabernet, all the have to do is buy some arsenic-laden insipid cabernet grapes from the Central Valley, vinify it into a reddish colored liquid that fits within the legal definition of wine, and slap a label on it that says ToKalon.

If you have any doubts about their plans to expand the use of the term, on March 24, 2017, Constellation filed to trademark “TO KALON VINEYARD COMPANY” and “TO KALON WINE COMPANY.”

Andy Beckstoffer may be replanting, and I have heard a variety of stories about his right to use the term ToKalon for one of his vineyards, but I do not know the correct story. I do know that his website terminology treats ToKalon as though it is a place where grapes are grown, but he may have a deal with the Mondavi Wine Company, now binding on Constellation, allowing him to use a trademark owned by someone else. There are other possible legal explanations, but it’s not my field. Maybe an intellectual property lawyer can butt in. Note that the Mondavi trademark (1988) pre-dates the acquisition of the vineyard by Beckstoffer (1993). The vineyard used to be owned by Beaulieu, according to Beckstoffer, but according to Cellartracker, one person owns (or used to own) a bottle of 1978 Robert Mondavi Winery Cabernet Sauvignon Reserve To-Kalon Vineyard.

Jay,

This is the best article that I know of on the twisted history and present status of To Kalon.

posts.cooked:
David Crow:

I’d be curious what people think are the “best” parts of To Kalon (per the bolded comment above), and what is it that makes them the best? I know Mondavi has some older plantings in some parts of the vineyard, but is it the old vines or the soils (or something else) that make those parts “better” than the Beckstoffer block?

Soil type and vine age. The best parts of To Kalon are the parts closest to the hills where the alluvial fan begins and there are more stones in the soil. Those are the sections owned by Detert, MacDonald, Mondavi, and Opus One. There are a larger percentage of stones in the soil in these sections. The MacDonald soils are about 50% stones. As you move away from the hills toward Hwy 29, the percentage of stones in the soil decreases. Alex MacDonald told me that the Beckstoffer block (former BV) has about 15% stones. Also, MacDonald has a small section of 60 year old vines (the oldest own rooted Cabernet in Napa along with Scarecrow), and 2 larger sections of 40 year old and 20 year old vines.

Enough already, and not meant as a criticism of John, who may not have read the rest of this thread, BUT THERE IS NO SUCH THING AS TOKALON FRUIT. ToKalon is a trademark that can be used by Constellation and it is not tied to a particular place. If it was a place name, it could not be trademarked. It’s like “Disneyland.” Is that a place in Anaheim or a trademark owned by Disney that they can use for an amusement park in Ulan Bator? The answer is choice #2!

While this is legally true at present, it is contested. After all, the Schrader-Beckstoffer-Mondavi lawsuit was settled out of court. And the outcome was that Beckstoffer and his clients were allowed to use the To Kalon name - hardly a victory for Mondavi or an endorsement of their trademark. So I would argue that the debate over whether To Kalon is a brand or a place is very far from being definitively settled in favor of the former. What would the outcome of another lawsuit be?

Correct. If Jay’s point is that there is no “To Kalon fruit” I think that is not true practically or legally. In a practical sense I think both Mondavi and Beckstoffer are following the vineyard designation rules which mandate that 90 or 95% of the fruit comes from that vineyard. I believe in the case of the 13 Mondavi To Kalon Rsv it is 100%. Legally iirc Beckstoffer was granted the right to use the To Kalon Vineyard designation but is bound to following the 90-95% rule. But THAT 90% IS To Kalon fruit and must come from the vineyard he owns which is geographically limited to the part which was the old To Kalon property. I don’t know if Mondavi was bound by the same limitation, probably not. But that does not mean that, to date, when they say To Kalon Vineyard or “fruit” it is not coming from that specific vineyard. The unsettled issue is whether they would be allowed to say To Kalon Vineyard and use outside grapes. Reliance on trademark only, probably says yes as Jay suggests. But there is a conflict with the vineyard designation rules. It seems a little strange that they would able to maintain that To Kalon means nothing geographically, but then attach “Vineyard” to not just a winery name, but also a vineyard designation, and still claim that there is no geographical definition of the vineyard as far as sourcing rules go.

The simplest solution is for Mondavi to maintain the trademark rights somthat no one else could establish To Kalon winery etc. Becks. Has the right to use To Kalon Vineyard and follow Vineyard designation but not expand use. Mondavi likewise claims To Kalon Vineyard as a dineyard designation or source definition only for grapes within their portion of the historical boundaries. So far that’s how it is done.

This is where we disagree. It is far from clear to me that it is in Constellation’s interest to be rigorous, and they are the ones who own the trademark. Rolls Royce sold just over 4,000 cars last year. If they could create a “second label” car and also use the Rolls Royce brand, and sell 400,000 cars of the second label, and make money with the second label, would the brand dilution of the first label cause its value to deteriorate so much that it would be a bad business decision? Apply that analysis to ToKalon. If Constellation starts marketing a $30 Cali Cab (which is above the ULTRA-ULTRA Premium price point) and calls it ToKalon and uses the brand name to hype sales, then a very popular $30 bottle will make them a lot more than a very limited production $200 bottle. I do not think there is anything to stop them, unless it is in the double secret probation settlement.

Well as another Central Valley participant, basking in this week’s 110°F temperatures, I must admit to not stepping foot outside to enjoy our wonderful sunsets.

I believe Constellation bought probably Napa’s most successful direct to consumer brand, and the trade secret of how to consistently get Parker 97-100 point scores year after year after year. If Constellation can figure out how Schrader do this, and can successfully apply this know how to their other high end brands they’ll be golden. On the other hand if Schrader suddenly loses the ability to get Parker 100 point scores the brand will be dead in three years or less.
